content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
PO BOX 807 · SACRAMENTO, 95804 · 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

May 26, 1977

Robert Olson
Executive Director
Seismic Safety Commission
455 Capitol Mall, Suite 535
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Mr. Olson:

Thank you for your letter of May 19, 1977, relating to the
potential necessity for the development of a Conflict of Interest
Code by the Seismic Safety Commission (Commission). The information contained in your letter indicates that it will be very
difficult to resolve the issue of whether the Commission is a
decision-making body within the meaning of the Fair Political
Practices Commission's (FPPC) regulations, 2 Cal. Adm. Code
§18700(a)(1) and within the meaning of the Political Reform
Act. (See, Government Code §82048).

Normally, the issue which you have presented would be raised
by a governmental agency after the agency had been notified for
the requirement of submission of a Conflict of Interest Code to
the FPPC in the context of a request for exemption. We can,
however, raise the issue for the Commission by formally noticing
the Commission for the submission of a Conflict of Interest Code
and entering an objection to the notice merely for the purpose
of raising the issue for the FPPC. In the event that the FPPC
determines that the notice is inadvertent based upon a hearing
in which the Commission would be a participant, either in the
position of an advocate or a source of information, the FPPC
may then dismiss the notice as improvidently issued.

With respect to the merits of the issue you raise, most
particularly, that involving the satisfaction of the requirements
of §71100(a)(1)(C), it appears that the relatively short life of
the Commission may preclude a finding that the recommendations
of the Commission have been "...over an extended period of time...
regularly approved without significant amendment or modification...." (See, 2 Cal. Adm. Code §18700(a)(1)(C). It was my
understanding at the time the FPPC adopted the regulation in
question, that the concept "extended period of time" comprised
a period somewhat longer than a year or even two. Furthermore,
the FPPC has not seen fit to describe an agency as "decision-

TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE ADMINISTRATION

Robert Olson
May 26, 1977
page two


making" for the purposes of compelling the development of a
Conflict of Interest Code solely on the basis of the satisfaction of §18700(a)(1)(C). Because of these reasons and
those set forth in your letter, it is my view that the FPPC
should fully consider the establishment of the requirement
to submit a Code by the Commission.

If you concur in this reasoning, please phone me and I
will formally notice the Commission for the submission of a
Conflict of Interest Code and ask that you raise an objection
or inquiry for the purposes of raising the issue so that the
matter may be placed on the Fair Political Practices Commission's calendar. The information which you have supplied
appears all that will be necessary to establish a record
in this matter.

If you have any questions on this matter, please feel
free to call me at 322-6444.

Sincerely,

[signature]
Dalbert L. Spurlock, Jr., Chief
Conflicts of Interest Division

DLS:mfa

STATE OF CALIFORNIA
SEISMIC SAFETY COMMISSION
455 Capitol Mall, Ste 535
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 322-4917

Robert A. Olsen,
Executive Director

EDMUND G. BROWN JR., Governor

May 19, 1977

Mr. Del Spurlock, Chief
Conflict of Interest Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
Post Office Box 807
1100 K Street Building
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Mr. Spurlock:

     I appreciated our recent discussion regarding potential
conflict of interest regulations for the Seismic Safety
Commission. I have enclosed a copy of the legislation creating
the Commission and our first Annual Report for your information.
As I mentioned, our Commissioners donate their time, which is
about two days a month.

     I have carefully read Sections 18700 through 18751.
Although the Seismic Safety Commission is advisory to both the
Legislature and the Governor, our actions and their subsequent
effects may fit the definition of "decision-making authority"
under the provisions of 18700(a)(1)(c), that section relating to
making "substantive recommendations". Further discussion with
you should help determine whether this is truly applicable.

     Also, I have studied the regulations relating to exemptions
and could possibly make the case that the Commission should be
exempted because of its advisory nature.

     The Chairman and I have discussed the general subject of
conflict of interest at some length, and there appear to be
several reasons why a conflict of interest regulation may be in
order. First, our law states that Commissioners work for the
public interest. Second, the Commission's work often involves
reviewing existing programs, policies, and practices of State and
local agencies, and this may result in changes in those programs.
Third, many Commissioners are in private practice and include
some of the best expertise available in the world in their fields.
As such, they may be contractors to State agencies for technical
work, and it may be that this is the very program being reviewed

Mr. Del Spurlock
May 19, 1977

Page 2


by the Commission at some point. Fourth, project issues arise
periodically, such as Auburn Dam, where members of the
Commission also sit as paid technical consultants to national
agencies, and it may raise questions if such Commissioners vote
on issues related to such projects that are before the Commission.
Last, some Commissioners are from the academic sector and
sometimes receive grants for earthquake research or sit on
technical advisory committees. Often, the potential funding
agencies, such as the National Science Foundation, solicit our
comments when reviewing research proposals leading to such grants
or ask for our comments on reports resulting from grants. From
time to time, Commissioners may have been involved with certain
specific projects.

Thus, I am seeking your advice on how best to proceed on
this matter. We wish to get the spirit of the law, protect the
integrity of the Commission, and assure as best we can that by
donating time to State service, a Commissioner's livelihood and
professional reputation are not jeopardized.

Thank you for your attention. I look forward to hearing
from you.

Sincerely,

ROBERT A. OLSON
Executive Director

RAO:jme

Enclosures: Seismic Safety Commission Law
Annual Report

cc: Geoffrey L. Graybill
Deputy Attorney General
